title: practical english usage - Part 365
content:
For to used instead of a whole infinitive (e.g. / don't ivant to, thanks), in comic strips.

like this in order to represent conversational pronunciation - for example In informal speech, want to often sounds like ‘ wanna’ . It is sometimes spelt 4 I wanna        hold     your      hand.

This coat wants cleaning. (= ... needs to be cleaned.) In this case, want can be followed by an -ing form (like need,  ►     532).

That car wants a clean. Your hair wants a good brush.

particularly with reference to actions.

In informal British English, we  can say that a thing ‘ wants’  (= needs) something, 3  want    meaning       'need' or                                              not I want you to be my friend, (   .. . as my friend.) (     / want you my friend;) To be or as is used before a noun complement.

I want her out of there now.

They wanted him  dead.

We want the Job finished by Tuesday.

She doesn't want him  back.

adverbial or past participle) to express ideas such as change or result.

Want can be followed by an object together with a complement (adjective, 2 structure       with    object     complement Do you want for me to make you some coffee?

+ object           + infinitive In American English, a structure with for         is also possible.

1 don't want that woman to come here.

Do you want me Io make you some cojjce? (          yutt waul (that) I make structure (►  98) is possible.

object           + infinitive 77i«r-clauses are not normally used after want, but an not I don't want to come back here ever again, (     J-don't-wanl come back...) After want, we  normally use an infinitive with to.

1  infinitive       with    to 618  want Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section     31 vocabulary •  620  way They tried ail possible methods of instruction, but the child learnt nothing.

The 19th century saw a revolution in means of transport.

We   use means of or method of instead.

Way of is unusual before a noun (except in the common expression way of life).

4 way     of   and   means       of There's no way to prove / of proving that he was stealing.

or of.. .ing. There is no important difference between the two structures.

After way (meaning 'method'/'manner') we  can use an infinitive structure 3  infinitive       or  -ing Let's go the way (that) we went yesterday.

I don't like the way (that) you talk to me.

In an informal style, we  often say the way (that) instead of the way in/by which.

2  relative      structures Do it (in) any way you like.      We went there the usual way.

You're doing it (in) the wrong way. Come this way.

In an informal style, we  usually drop the prepositions in or by before way.

1  preposition        dropped 620 way ►     575 and watch,      look     (at)     see in American English, toward and afterward are also common.

Towards and afterwards are the usual forms in British English; 3 other     words She put forward a very interesting suggestion.

/ look forward to hearing from you.

put forward, the form without -s is always used.

In some figurative expressions such as look forward to, bring forward, Let's start driving homeward(s).

If we keep going upward(s), we must get to the top.

Why are you moving baekward(s) and forward(s)?

and the forms without -s in American English.

The  forms with -s are generally a little more common in British English, When these words are adverbs, they can generally be used with or without -s.

He was last seen driving in a northward direction.

You're not allowed to make a forward pass in rugby.

This country is very backward in some ways.

When  they are used as adjectives, they do not have -s.

vocabulary  •  622  well say, for example, She’s a well girl.                       -> Well is not common before a noun. We   can say She’s well, but it is less usual to not When I’m  with you I'm happy. (     When I'm with you I'm well.) When I’m  in the mountains I am always well.

Note that the adjective well is only used to talk about health. Compare: ‘ How are you? ‘ Quite well, thanks.'  I don't feel very well.

There is also an adjective well, meaning ‘ in good health'.
